coredata.dc:title: Bitter apple peel derived photoactive carbon dots for the sunlight induced photocatalytic degradation of crystal violet dye
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2020 International Solar Energy Society. Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: International Solar Energy Society. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Aggarwal, Ruchi
Saini, Deepika
Singh, Buta
Kaushik, Jaidev
Garg, Anjali Kumari
Sonkar, Sumit Kumar
coredata.dc:description: A simple and sustainable approach is described for the synthesis of photoactive carbon dots (CD) as an efficient photo-catalytic material. The photoactive-CD has been fabricated by simply charring the peels of Bitter Apple (BA). It is somewhat similar to the biochar synthesis. The photoactivity of BA peel derived photoactive-CD was tested under the illumination of sunlight for photodegradation of a pollutant organic dye, crystal violet (CV). A possible mechanism has been proposed for photodegradation of dye based on the trap experiment, where electron and holes were majorly responsible. BA peel derived photoactive-CD showed remarkable results concerning the photodegradation of CV (20 ppm in ~ 90 min) under sunlight illumination as compared to the dark conditions.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Waste Bitter Apple peel
Carbon Dots
Crystal violet
Photocatalysis
Photodegradation
Photoactive material

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) XPS full survey scan spectra; (b) percentage elemental composition; short scan of (c) C1s; (d) O1s; (e) N1s; and (f) Raman spectra of photoactive-CD.
(a) Low resolution TEM image of photoactive-CD; (b) HRTEM image of photoactive-CD showing the graphitic fringes; and (c) corresponding zoomed image of (b) showing the marked fringes.

(a) Effect of catalyst photoactive-CD on degradation of CV dye under different condition; (b) Plot of (C/Co) vs time for photocatalytic degradation of different concentration of CV dye; (c) pseudo first order linear fit data with different concentration of CV dye; (d) Effect of different amount of catalyst loading on photocatalytic degradation of CV dye; (e) Trap experiment based on different scavengers; (f) Cartoon illustration showing the possibe degradation mechanism; (g) Plausible mechanism depicted by reactions involved in the photodegradation; and (h) Recycle study of the photoactive-CD.
(a) Digital photograph of CV photodegradation; (b) Chemical structure of CV; (c) Comparative 1H NMR spectra of the CV (control dye) with treated wastewater (photodegradation of CV).
Schematic representation showing the fabrication of the photoactive-CD from dried BA peels.
Comparative analysis of removal of toxic CV based on different types of catalysts.

row.entry: Zinc chloride activated (RHZ) rice husk carbon

head.ce:title: Bitter apple peel derived photoactive carbon dots for the sunlight induced photocatalytic degradation of crystal violet dye

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Malaviya National Institute of Technology, Jaipur, Jaipur 302017, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Malaviya National Institute of Technology, Jaipur

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Malaviya National Institute of Technology, Jaipur, Jaipur 302017, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A simple and sustainable approach is described for the synthesis of photoactive carbon dots (CD) as an efficient photo-catalytic material. The photoactive-CD has been fabricated by simply charring the peels of Bitter Apple (BA). It is somewhat similar to the biochar synthesis. The photoactivity of BA peel derived photoactive-CD was tested under the illumination of sunlight for photodegradation of a pollutant organic dye, crystal violet (CV). A possible mechanism has been proposed for photodegradation of dye based on the trap experiment, where electron and holes were majorly responsible. BA peel derived photoactive-CD showed remarkable results concerning the photodegradation of CV (20 ppm in ~ 90 min) under sunlight illumination as compared to the dark conditions.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CD) (LeCroy et al., 2014; Sun et al., 2006) generally consists of a carbonaceous core which is majorly surrounded by the oxygenated surface groups (Alves et al., 2016). As well the surface functionalization can easily be achieved via the process of functionalization (Sudolská et al., 2015) and passivation (Cao et al., 2007) which significantly improves the photophysical properties of CD. Because of their unique surface chemistry, environmental sustainability and low-toxicity, CD are highly compatible with the aqueous chemistry and biomedical applications. Presently, CD have been fabricated via a lot of unconventional ways, and the simplest way is the utilization of organic bio-waste (Meng et al., 2019) concerning its carbonization/ pyrolysis. For example, CD were reported from the waste lemon peels (Tyagi et al., 2016), orange juice (Sahu et al., 2012), plant extract (Khare et al., 2018), sugarcane bagasse (Sharma et al., 2019), peanut skin or shell (Saxena and Sarkar, 2012), hair-derived CD (Liu et al., 2014), lychee seeds (Xue et al., 2015), watermelon peel (Zhou et al., 2012), wood wool (Sonkar et al., 2012), food waste (Park et al., 2014) and so on. For the applicative perspectives, apart from being utilized in conventional imaging and sensing applications (Bhati et al., 2018; He et al., 2016), these are currently been employed in the field of photocatalysis (Ramírez-Aparicio et al., 2016a). Hence, could uphold a promising future in the sunlight induced (Ramírez-Aparicio et al., 2016b) activities like photoreduction (Bhati et al., 2019) of toxic metal ions and the photodegradation of organic dyes (Gupta and Pandey, 2019; Saini et al., 2019). An increase in global population increases the discharge of a wide variety of organic pollutants in the environment (Jain et al., 2019), such as industrial effluent containing textile dyes which is one of the primary sources of environmental pollution (Dasari and Guttena, 2019). Among all effluent discharges from industry, one of the widely used organic dye (Smazna et al., 2019; Gupta and Pandey, 2019) having an organic structure of triphenylmethane, is crystal violet (CV), very well known to cause several health issues. Like irritation to eyes, respiratory tract and gastro intestinal tract (Kanagamani et al., 2019). Their removal from waste effluents are tough, due to their high degree of organic structural complexity. Many techniques are presently in use including adsorption (Qin et al., 2015), biodegradation (Almeida and Corso, 2014), flocculation (Guibal and Roussy, 2007), ultrafiltration (Simonič, 2009), etc. for the treatment of CV in wastewater. While using these expensive physiochemical methods, a vast amount of sludge is produced, this results in a secondary level of land pollution. For the same, there is an urgent need to explore an inexpensive, eco-friendly technique for the removal of the organic pollutant dyes (Khalilian et al., 2015).

In this report a simple approach for the isolation of photoactive-CD from the carbonization of dried peels of bitter apple (BA) is reported. BA is having a wide medicinal importance (Gurudeeban et al., 2010; Hussain et al., 2014; Liu et al., 2018) and herein its waste peels has been used for the photodegradation of toxic CV dye under the presence of sunlight. Being insoluble in nature photoactive-CD has been easily separated from the photodegraded CV water and hence been used for multiple cycles.

The overall process for the fabrication of photoactive-CD has been illustrated in Scheme 1 . The dried peels of BA fruit were grounded into a fine yellow powder using the mortar-pestle. Further this yellow fine powder was carbonized in the muffle furnace at ~ 300 °C for 2 h. The as prepared charred black carbon powder was termed as photoactive-CD, and been used for the photocatalytic application after sonication. The ease of availability of BA peels (generally discarded as waste) makes the whole process extremely economical for the fabrication of photoactive-CD, used for the photodegradation of the organic dye as CV under the illumination of sunlight.

The surface functionalities and graphitic nature of photoactive-CD was analyzed by X-ray Photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and Raman spectroscopy respectively as shown in Fig. 1 . XPS full survey scan spectra in Fig. 1(a) showed three major peaks confirming the presence of C, O and N on the surface of photoactive-CD. The percentage elemental composition of C, O and N was found to be ~ 60.30%, ~ 37.47% and ~ 2.23% respectively, (Fig. 1(b)). The short scan spectra of C1s in Fig. 1(c) depicts two types of peaks at ~ 284.41 eV corresponding to C-C (Anand et al., 2019) and ~ 285.52 eV corresponding to C-O/N bond. In Fig. 1(d), short scan spectra of O1s represents O-N/C (Singh et al., 2017) bonding at ~ 531.35 eV and COO– bonding at ~ 533.3 eV. The short scan spectra of N1s is displayed in Fig. 1(e) shows two types of bonding, one is –C–N at ~ 398.44 eV and another is –C=N bond at ~ 400.3 eV. Fig. 1(f) showed the graphitic constituent of photoactive-CD via Raman analysis where the presence of graphitic band (G band) and disordered bands (D band) at ~ 1571 cm−1 and ~ 1355 cm−1 respectively. G-band was recognized due to sp2 hybridization and D-band results due to sp3 hybridized carbon atoms present on the surface of photoactive-CD (Dubey et al., 2015).

The morphology and interplanar arrangement of photoactive-CD was characterized by low resolution Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM). Fig. 2 (a) shows the TEM image of spherically shaped photoactive-CD. Fig. 2(b) displays the HRTEM image of photoactive-CD showing the presence of graphitic fringes encircled with red color along with its zoomed image as Fig. 2(c) showing its d-spacing of ~ 0.27 nm marked with yellow lines.

Fig. 4 (a) represent the digital photographic image of CV before and after the photodegradation by photoactive-CD under the illumination of sunlight. The process of the photodegradation of CV by photoactive-CD was supported by NMR analysis. Fig. 4(b) showing the chemical structure of CV dye molecule. Fig. 4(c) shows the comparative aqueous phase 1H NMR analysis of CV as a control sample with respect to photodegraded products of CV. The NMR spectra of CV was taken before and after the photodegradation (samples collected from supernatant pool of dye-photoactive-CD system) then dried and redissolved in D2O (for the 1H NMR analysis). Fig. 4(c), shows the proton peaks associated with the aliphatic and aromatic region of CV dye. 1H NMR of CV shows peak at 3.15 ppm for H1 protons, 6.8 ppm for H2 protons and at 7.22 ppm for H3 marked protons. Over a comparative analysis, in treated wastewater (after photodegradation) breakage of the aromatic framework of the CV dye was supported by the disappearance of the signals in the aromatic region. As well as, the 1H NMR spectrum of the photodegraded CV (treated wastewater) show the appearance of new smaller aliphatic peaks (multiplet, fragmented hydrocarbons), which can be directly related to the disintegration of original aromatic organic framework of CV molecules into the smaller aliphatic fragments (Singh et al., 2019).

The photoactive-CD were prepared via single step carbonization method by using waste BA peels. This procedure is facile and feasible for large scale production. The photoactive-CD were used as an effective photoactive material utilized in sunlight-driven photodegradation of toxic CV dye. The mechanism governing dye degradation was investigated by active species scavenging experiment, where holes and electrons were identified as an active species responsible for the photodegradation. The complex organic aromatic framework of CV breaks into smaller aliphatic fragments as confirmed by the NMR analysis. Interestingly, further studies of this type of photoactive-CD may reveal a vast prospective in the field of photocatalysis and water treatment.

Photoactive-CD was prepared by the carbonization of the dried peels of BA. Dried peels of BA were ground to a fine yellow powder using the mortar-pestle, followed by its carbonization in a muffle furnace at ~ 300 °C for 2 h. The as produced black carbon powder was termed as photoactive-CD, and been used as photoactive material in CV degradation after the sonication to create its suspension. Photocatalytic degradation of CV was analyzed by the interaction of dispersed photoactive-CD in aqueous media upon irradiation of sunlight. A stock solution of 20 ppm CV was prepared in distilled water for the photocatalysis experiment. In a simpler methodology, 10 mg of photoactive-CD was dispersed in 50 ml of CV solution via sonication for about 10 min to reach the adsorption-desorption equilibrium. Then these solutions were placed under the illumination of sunlight. After every 10 min interval, the photo reacted aliquots (1 ml) were collected and centrifuged. This solution was diluted and UV–Vis absorbance was noted at wavelength 589 nm for the determination of CV concentration. The photodegraded CV sample that was used for the UV–Vis analysis, was dried on water bath and dissolved in D2O solvent for the 1H NMR analysis to understand in detail about the breakage of organic framework of CV molecules.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: All Authors declares no conflict of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: R.A. thanks MNIT Jaipur for doctoral fellowship, D.S. thanks Department of Science and Technology (DST), India (IF180133) for Inspire doctoral fellowship, J.K. thanks MNIT Jaipur for doctoral fellowship, A.K.G. thanks Council of Scientific and Industrial Research (CSIR), India for doctoral fellowship and S.K.S. thanks DST, India (SB/EMEQ-383/2014) for funding. S. K. S. thanks Material Research Centre (MRC), MNIT Jaipur, India for material characterization.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Photoluminescent green carbon nanodots from food-waste-derived sources: large-scale synthesis, properties, and biomedical applications
